item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image comes from here and is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki’s license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep that's a fair assumption. I thought about mentioning its durability in passing, but I felt it would make the piece sound too much like a list of facts and reduce the effectiveness of the imagery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you all for your comments. After much thought and deliberation, I've changed things up, principally by replacing the discovery with an incident that I hope packs sufficient punch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, seconding this. It basically feels Ophite-lite, which — while still not bad — only really reminds me of how good stuff like this can be. It needs a little bit more of something, but I'll be damned if I know what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just getting really into the site again now after spending several years away from it, and I just want to say - this is a fantastic new SCP. :)

I have one quibble, but it's minor - can we assume that it heals itself quickly? I don't see any explicit reference to it, but I figure that if it didn't, natural wear and tear would do it in. This is, of course, hardly the most interesting part of a human-esophagus-based subway system made by and for rodents, but it does still make you wonder!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The page is only about a day old. Sometimes authors wait a bit before naming pages, to make sure they're happy with it staying as currently posted.

ETA: also, typically if you want to catch an author's attention, a direct wikidot PM is the way to go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept here is nice, but it’s not developed to its full potential. It lacks an evenly spaced elbows moment. Something that snaps the reader out of the cold clinical description and emphasizes how truly strange the esophagus rat subway is.

The pieces are here but there’s nothing memorable here and that makes me sad. Giving a little more personality to the living throat might be a start. It’s curious how right now it has nothing to say.

I could take or leave the last addenda, it feels like a bit of an info dump right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You forgot to put up a title by the number. On the Series V page

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello! Thanks for the critique, I really appreciate it.

I was intending to convey the weird and often repulsive relationship between humans and synanthropes through the imagery of a giant human esophagus which serves as a kind of express highway for rodents. In that sense, it's meant to be treated as somewhat innocuous, in a sense fitting in with the rest of the city's infrastructure. Disgust is what I was aiming for here.

+ show block
– hide block

I'll implement changes based on your comments, but I'll also explain my thought process behind some of the things you pointed out.

Small mammals using it as a means of travel but remaining limp while within the SCP feels a little weird here? Are you intending to convey that it's kinda like a futurama style people tube rather than like "the animals run across it"?

Yeah, the Futurama-style people tube is an apt analogy. The rodents are taking advantage of the muscle contractions( i.e. peristalsis) of the skip for movement. So they themselves don't need to move.

It feels kinda weird that for about 30 years, the Foundation knew this existed but didn't use radar to track it? From what I can tell, ground-penetrating radar has existed since 1910 and like, there were developments in it during the 70s which meant it really started to take off then?

You're right, but as far as I know, it would probably still be pretty difficult to use modern GPR to track something with the diameter of a human esophagus in an area already saturated with all sorts of piping and tunnels and whatnot. That's why I chose 2003 as a date when the Foundation had effectively developed specialised GPR allowing them to track the skip. But I realise it's not made clear at all here.

….Honestly part of me feels like something more evocative should be used here? This is very, dry and factual and it feels like an opportunity to really convey…something.

Fair enough. I'll think about writing a weirder discovery log. I was attempting to imply that a concentration of rodents in one location, in this case the guy's absurd number of pet mice, attracts the skip to that location. Mayhaps that's not that interesting of an implication though. I'll think of something else.

Thanks again for the feedback.

Edit: I've implemented several changes based on your feedback. I hope it makes for clearer and more impactful reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm. I kinda really dig the concept here and there's a lot of imagery and potential; but it kinda feels like this doesn't quite do enough with the idea here? I'm really not sure how I'm expected to feel about this SCP: generally a huge organism living under a city should be bad but it seems kinda benign; and it has some connection with sewage so maybe it's like eating sewage or something and could be good? I don't really feel terrified or disgusted by this or really anything at all.

It kinda feels like it needs more: some kind of stakes or indication of what the consequences of this thing existing are? Possibly some kind of exploration of what's inside it so you can really convey the horror/weirdness of it?

+ minor things
– hide block

Non-Foundational personnel and civilians observing or otherwise interacting with SCP-4594 are to be amnesticised where necessary.

I'm not generally a fan of vague phrasing like "where necessary" in the ConProcs: like, to me, the ConProcs should have some indication of when it's necessary to amnesticise someone who interacts with the SCP: is it just if they realise it's not regular plumbing? is it just if they're posting about the SCP's existence a lot on social media?

across, and is limited to, the area of Greater London.

Hm. The phrasing "is limited to" implies "it can't leave Greater London" but it seems like it could if it wanted to, it just doesn't want to?

SCP-4594’s interior is host to an indeterminate number of rodents and other small mammals that use the object as a means of travel.

SCP-4594’s occupants are docile and do not actively interact with one another, instead remaining limp while within SCP-4594.

Small mammals using it as a means of travel but remaining limp while within the SCP feels a little weird here? Are you intending to convey that it's kinda like a futurama style people tube rather than like "the animals run across it"?

Since 2003, the application of specialized ground-penetrating radar has enabled the Foundation

It feels kinda weird that for about 30 years, the Foundation knew this existed but didn't use radar to track it? From what I can tell, ground-penetrating radar has existed since 1910 and like, there were developments in it during the 70s which meant it really started to take off then?

was found lodged between the plumbing in a way

Something about the phrasing here feels, a little clunky? I think because normally "between" features two things ("between a rock and a hard place") and here you just have one (the plumbing): perhaps "around" or "within" would work better than between?

….Honestly part of me feels like something more evocative should be used here? This is very, dry and factual and it feels like an opportunity to really convey…something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No particular reason. I just wanted to indicate how the Foundation gathered much of their info on the skip in the past, but I can see that it weirdly stands out. I'll make some edits to indicate that the explorations are ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why has exploration been discontinued since 2017, I saw that in the conprocs but then didn’t see anything in the article to explain (unless I missed it)
